Q1: I tried to keep a somewhat consistent routine, usually studying in the afternoons when I could focus better. I'd aim for about three hours a day spread across the week, listening to lecture recordings while taking notes. Honestly though, my anxiety sometimes made it hard to stick to the plan, and I'd end up reorganizing my schedule more than actually studying. I found that having a rough structure helped me feel less overwhelmed, even if I didn't always follow it perfectly.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I'd try to maintain regular study times early in the week, but as deadlines approached, everything would shift into crisis mode. My stress levels would spike and I'd end up pulling longer sessions right before submissions. It wasn't ideal, but with ten online courses to juggle, sometimes that was the only way to manage everything.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was coming up or when I got a notification about new content. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed because the anxiety of falling behind was worse than actually looking. The peer support group I was part of would sometimes remind each other about deadlines, which helped prompt me to check in more regularly.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning with my coffee became somewhat automatic. I think it happened because I was afraid of missing something important, so the habit formed out of anxiety more than anything else. Once I connected with a few peers through the discussion forums, I'd also log in to see if anyone had responded to my posts. It wasn't exactly a comfortable routine, but it was a routine nonetheless.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often replay sections multiple times until concepts clicked. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing how other students understood the material gave me different perspectives. When my peer support connections were active, those interactions really helped clarify things I was struggling with.
Q6: I honestly ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With my auditory learning style, reading dense text just didn't stick the same way. I'd skim them at best or skip them entirely if there was a video alternative. Some of the optional quizzes I also avoided because they felt like extra pressure when I was already stressed about the required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually hear the information being explained, or if it directly connected to an upcoming assessment. If I could tell it would help my exam performance or assignment grades, I'd prioritize it. Group discussions with peers also felt valuable because talking through problems helped me process information better than just reading about it.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With my anxiety already high about completing required work, adding optional tasks just seemed like inviting more stress. Required activities had clear consequences, so they got my attention first. Sometimes I'd look at optional resources and think 'maybe later,' but later rarely came because I was always catching up on something else.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download lecture audio and listen while commuting or doing chores around my apartment. I also recorded myself summarizing key concepts and played those back repeatedly. Sometimes I'd call a friend from my peer support group and we'd discuss course material for an hour, which was incredibly helpful but completely invisible to the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be actively learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd listen to downloaded lectures on my phone while walking, or I'd be reviewing handwritten notes I'd made from earlier sessions. Some of my best study sessions happened when I was talking through concepts with peers over the phone or in person. The LMS only saw me download something once, not the five times I reviewed it offline.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd access a video lecture once, download it or take detailed audio notes, and then study from those materials repeatedly offline. My internet is reliable enough, but I found it easier to focus without the distraction of being online. The LMS would show one view, but I might have engaged with that content a dozen times in reality.
Q12: Mid-semester was particularly brutal when multiple assignments converged at once. My anxiety would spike to the point where I'd freeze up and struggle to start anything. There were also personal things happening with family that pulled my attention away from coursework. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt overwhelming because I knew I'd see all the things I was behind on.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities, especially when my stress was high, meant I'd sometimes access materials but not have the mental energy to actually engage with them. My assignment completion rate probably reflects those periods where I was technically present but mentally checked out.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be racing about other deadlines or personal worries. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. It felt like I was doing something productive, but looking back, I wasn't actually absorbing much during those sessions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew the deadline was approaching. My anxiety would make me avoid starting because I was afraid of doing it wrong or not doing well enough. The irony is that avoiding it made my anxiety worse, but in the moment, not facing it felt like the safer option. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow when I felt better, but that better day rarely came.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be relatively calm early on, then as deadlines approached, my login frequency would spike dramatically. The pressure of a deadline was often the only thing that could override my avoidance tendencies. It wasn't healthy, but that urgency was sometimes the only thing that got me to actually submit work.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities, but critical feedback sometimes made me avoid that area entirely for a while. I appreciated when instructors gave constructive comments, but my anxiety would sometimes blow negative feedback out of proportion. The peer feedback I got through my support group was usually easier to hear.
Q18: The course structure with clear weekly modules helped me know what to expect, which reduced some anxiety. However, having ten online courses meant the structures sometimes conflicted with each other, making it hard to prioritize. When the course had flexible deadlines, I'd often push things back, which wasn't always good for my completion rate. More rigid structures were stressful but kept me on track.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. However, when important information was only in text format, I struggled more. The discussion forums were designed well and made peer interaction feel natural. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks to track – my anxiety would fixate on the possibility of missing something.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. I'd log in frequently, watch videos, participate in discussions, but then struggle to actually complete and submit assignments. The anxiety of submitting imperfect work was sometimes worse than the anxiety of not submitting at all. High activity can mean someone is trying hard but hitting psychological barriers when it comes to producing final work.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much learning happens offline. Someone could download everything in one session, study intensively from those materials, and perform well on exams without much ongoing LMS activity. I know peers who barely touched the LMS but aced exams because they studied from textbooks or formed study groups. The system doesn't see the real work happening.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone is cramming before deadlines or how often they access certain resources. But they completely miss the quality of engagement – whether someone is actually learning or just clicking through. They can't capture offline study, peer conversations, or the mental effort someone puts into understanding material. They also can't show when someone is too anxious to engage even though they're trying.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, making connections, and caring about understanding it properly. It's not just about logging in or watching videos – it's about my mind being present and curious. For me, engagement often happens when I'm discussing concepts with peers or when I'm replaying a lecture and suddenly something clicks. It's an internal state that external metrics can't fully capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected my engagement, or how peer support conversations helped me stay motivated. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing other things, or the mental energy I invested in trying to overcome avoidance. It also wouldn't show that despite my struggles with assignment completion, I genuinely cared about learning the material and worked hard in ways that aren't easily measured.
